why: why parent click fires when child is clicked
why does my click event trigger twice
output: click event fires on parent after child

1_beginner_explanation.summary: **Event Bubbling** is the third and most famous phase of Event Propagation. After an event hits its target element, it 'bubbles' up through all of its parent elements, all the way to the `document`. If any of those parent elements have an event listener for that same event, they will trigger too. This is the default behavior in all modern browsers.
1_beginner_explanation.analogy: Imagine you are holding an underwater balloon (the Event) at the bottom of a pool (the target element) and you let it go. It floats straight up to the surface (the `document`), passing every layer of water (parent elements) on its way up.

2_internal_mechanism.execution_flow: During bubbling, the engine checks the target element's parent, then its grandparent, all the way to `window`. For each node, if there is a listener attached for that event type (and it wasn't registered with `capture: true`), the engine pushes its callback onto the Call Stack.
2_internal_mechanism.memory: No new memory is allocated during bubbling; the engine just traverses the existing C++ DOM parent pointers.

3_real_world_example.use_case: Understanding why clicking a child triggers the parent.

4_common_confusion.trap: Thinking that `e.target` changes as the event bubbles up. It does NOT. `e.target` is forever locked to the deepest element that originated the event. If you want to know which parent the event is currently bubbling through, you must use `e.currentTarget`.
4_common_confusion.fix: target = who started it. currentTarget = who is handling it right now.

6_edge_case.quirk: If you absolutely must stop bubbling immediately and prevent even OTHER listeners on the EXACT SAME element from running, you use `e.stopImmediatePropagation()`.
6_edge_case.code:
btn.addEventListener('click', (e) => { e.stopImmediatePropagation(); });
btn.addEventListener('click', () => { console.log('I will never run'); });
